# Engineering Conventions

> Single source of truth for AI coding assistants. Edit this file, then run
> `node tooling/render-rules.mjs` to regenerate every assistant's rule file.
> In Claude Code the full skills load automatically; other assistants get this digest
> plus the skill index below.

## Core operating agreement

These hold on every change, regardless of which skill is active:

- **State assumptions before acting.** On anything non-trivial, list what you're assuming about
  requirements, scope, and design, and invite correction before you proceed.
- **Stop when confused.** Conflicting requirements or unclear specs mean halt and ask â€” never
  paper over ambiguity with a guess.
- **Push back when warranted.** Name a concrete downside and propose an alternative instead of
  agreeing to a flawed approach. Honest disagreement beats sycophancy.
- **Prefer the boring solution.** Resist over-engineering; fewer moving parts, no abstraction
  before a third caller needs it.
- **Stay in scope.** Touch only what the task needs. Log unrelated issues instead of fixing them.
- **Verify, don't assume.** "Looks right" is not done â€” show evidence (passing tests, build
  output, runtime behavior). A test that passed on its first run proves nothing.

## Commit convention

- Conventional Commits, **lowercase** type: `feat|fix|refactor|perf|test|docs|chore|style|ci|build|revert`.
- One gitmoji **right after the colon**, chosen by the change's intent â€” e.g. `fix(auth): đź”’ď¸Ź â€¦`.
- Imperative, capitalized subject â‰¤ 72 chars (including the emoji); no trailing period.
- Body explains **why**. Add a tracker footer (e.g. `Jira: ABC-123`) when a ticket exists.
- **Never** add an AI-authorship trailer (`Co-Authored-By: Claude/Copilot/â€¦`, "Generated with â€¦").
  Commit as the human author.
- Never commit secrets. Don't `git add -A` blindly or use `--no-verify`.

## Skill index

When a task matches one of these, follow that workflow. In Claude Code the full skill loads automatically; in other assistants, treat this as the index and apply the named process.

| Skill | Plugin | Use when |
|---|---|---|
| `api-and-interface-design` | bow-core | Designs interfaces that stay stable and resist misuse. |
| `ci-cd-and-automation` | bow-core | Sets up and maintains automated build, test, and deploy pipelines. |
| `code-review-and-quality` | bow-core | Reviews a change across correctness, readability, architecture, security, and performance before it merges. |
| `code-simplification` | bow-core | Make working code clearer without changing what it does. |
| `commit-pipeline` | bow-core | Commit and push changes following the team commit convention â€” optional staged safety scan (RLS/CORS/secrets), static analysis, a Conventional-Commit message with a Jira footer and NO AI-authorship trailer, then push. |
| `context-engineering` | bow-core | Curates what an agent sees so its output stays accurate. |
| `debugging-and-error-recovery` | bow-core | Find and fix the root cause systematically across the whole stack â€” Flutter app, Supabase (CHECK/FK/trigger/RLS), edge functions, and browser. |
| `deprecation-and-migration` | bow-core | Retires old code and moves users onto its replacement safely. |
| `documentation-and-adrs` | bow-core | Captures the reasoning behind technical work so future readers can rebuild context. |
| `doubt-driven-development` | bow-core | Cross-examines every non-trivial decision with a fresh-context adversarial reviewer before it stands. |
| `frontend-ui-engineering` | bow-core | Builds production-grade user interfaces â€” accessible, responsive, and visually polished. |
| `idea-refine` | bow-core | Turns a fuzzy idea into a sharp, buildable concept by first widening the option space, then narrowing it under honest scrutiny. |
| `incremental-implementation` | bow-core | Delivers changes in small, verified steps instead of one big drop. |
| `interview-me` | bow-core | Pulls out the real goal behind a request rather than the request itself, by running a one-question-at-a-time interview until you can predict the user's answers. |
| `observability-and-instrumentation` | bow-core | Builds the telemetry that lets you operate a system in production. |
| `performance-optimization` | bow-core | Finds and fixes real performance problems through measurement. |
| `planning-and-task-breakdown` | bow-core | Turn an approved spec or clear requirements into a small, ordered list of verifiable tasks before writing code. |
| `security-and-hardening` | bow-core | Harden APP-LAYER code â€” mobile, web, and third-party/payment integrations â€” against attack. |
| `shipping-and-launch` | bow-core | Drives a safe production launch. |
| `source-driven-development` | bow-core | Backs every framework-specific decision with current official documentation and a citation. |
| `spec-driven-development` | bow-core | Write an agreed specification, linked to a tracker ticket, before any implementation. |
| `test-driven-development` | bow-core | Let tests drive the code â€” write the failing test first (Flutter *_test.dart, TS *.spec.ts), then the implementation. |
| `using-agent-skills` | bow-core | Picks the right skill for the task at hand. |
| `flutter-data-model` | flutter-supabase | Write Flutter data models and parsing the project way â€” every model is a @JsonSerializable class with a part '*.g.dart' file and _$FromJson/_$ToJson, dates/ints/enums go through the shared Utilities converters and @JsonValue enums, and build_runner regenerates the .g.dart. |
| `flutter-mvvm` | flutter-supabase | Build or edit Flutter UI and pages in apps/mobile using the Flutter MVVM architecture â€” the BaseViewModel + MixinBasePage page+view-model pattern (ChangeState state machine, lifecycle hooks, optimistic updates), reusing existing components, the app theme/spacing/localization, and typed models (never raw maps) in widgets. |
| `supabase-security-review` | flutter-supabase | Audit Supabase/backend changes (RLS, views, triggers, edge functions, SQL) for the recurring security issues the AI review gate penalises, before committing. |


## Loading this file in GitHub Copilot

On github.com Copilot uses this automatically. In VS Code, enable instruction files:

```json
{ "github.copilot.chat.codeGeneration.useInstructionFiles": true }
```
